articles.title: New Prime Minister, Same Problem
articles.content:
2024年7月，英国首相基尔·斯塔默带领工党取得压倒性胜利时，他将这一结果誉为终结保守党十四年失政的开端，预示着一个长期稳定的技术官僚政府时代的到来。斯塔默这位谨慎的前皇家检察署署长，有意将自己与保守党首相鲍里斯·约翰逊在新冠疫情期间违规聚会以及其继任者莉兹·特拉斯引发的金融崩溃形成对比，承诺回归严谨作风，而财政大臣蕾切尔·里夫斯则誓言将财政审慎与更积极的政府角色相结合。疲惫的选民给了工党33.7%的温和支持率，由于选票分布方式，这转化为下议院650个席位中的411个。这算不上强有力的背书——立即被戏称为“缺乏热爱的压倒性胜利”——但足以让斯塔默获得暂缓质疑的信任。然而近两年后，选民的疑虑已演变为近乎蔑视。工党在大多数全国民调中落后于奈杰尔·法拉奇的激进改革英国党近十个百分点，这与2024年大选时改革党的得票率落后工党近二十个百分点形成鲜明对比。6月22日，斯塔默的处境已难以为继，他向现实低头，宣布一旦工党选出继任者，他将辞去职务。几乎确定无疑的继任者是安迪·伯纳姆，这位深受欢迎的曼彻斯特大区区前市长，在6月18日通过补选以近55%的得票率赢得迈基菲尔德选区议席后进入议会。

对工党困境的大多数解释都集中在策略失误上：斯塔默的沟通失败、里夫斯过于严格的财政规则、2024年削减老年人冬季燃料补贴的决定，以及2025年征收的家庭农场税。这些都是真实存在的，但它们是症状而非根源。更深层的故事是，脱欧——现代英国史上最具定义性的政治事件——终于开始呈现它的账单，而工党恰好在此刻执政。近十年来，联合王国成功推迟了对2016年6月脱欧公投的全面清算。斯塔默的策略依赖于将脱欧问题去政治化，消除其作为活跃政治议题的地位，使其既不能团结他的对手，也不能分裂他自身的选举联盟。这意味着排除关税同盟或单一市场成员资格的可能性，并将与欧盟之间有限的监管合作包装为他的最大抱负。在选举层面，这一策略是有效的。它在短期内没有付出代价，在2024年相对宽容的环境下，英国经济虽然表现逊于同行，但尚能避免急性危机。那种环境已不复存在。唐纳德·特朗普重返白宫、他对关税的武器化运用，以及以规则为基础的贸易秩序的崩溃，暴露了一个已无缓冲余地的英国经济。欧盟尽管存在种种 dysfunction（功能障碍），但拥有庞大的内部市场，具备与大国谈判的规模，而不仅仅是作为一个乞求者的角色。联合王国则不具备这种地位。现在英国应对全球风暴的最佳方式——也是工党维持其联盟不解体的最佳方式——是与欧盟寻求更紧密的合作。有理由相信一项重大协议是可能的。英国经济需要进入欧洲市场，而欧洲则需要英国的军事力量。所需要的是最后一种难以企及的要素：政治意愿。

要理解为什么英国比其欧洲邻国更深受近期经济冲击之苦，回顾欧盟成员资格曾提供什么会有所帮助。单一市场让英国企业无障碍地进入4.5亿消费者市场，使英国成为寻求在欧洲立足的外国投资的磁石。关税同盟使英国出口免受跨越英吉利海峡货物现在所面临的原产地规则检查、卫生检疫和繁文缛节。允许欧盟企业在欧盟任何地方运营的特权——即所谓的“通行证”制度——使伦敦金融城成为欧洲大陆事实上的金融中心，而协调监管则让英国在制定欧洲标准时拥有发言权。所有这些都无需英国放弃自己的货币、央行、财政政策或军队。约翰·梅杰首相在九十年代初的马斯特里赫特条约谈判中为英国争取到的 opt-outs（选择退出权），使国家获得了整合的大部分好处，而几乎没有超国家层面的约束。这是欧洲其他国家所容忍的，因为英国带来了军事力量、金融专业知识，以及对法德主导地位的制衡。离开这一安排的决定，正如马克·布赖斯和我在两年前的《外交事务》杂志上所论证的，是一个巨大的经济乌龙球。非关税壁垒的引入使与欧洲贸易的成本增加了约4%的长期国内生产总值——这是英国政府资助的预算责任办公室目前的估算。这意味着每年永久性地减少约1320亿美元的国家收入，接近每个家庭平均减少4650美元。英国对欧盟的服务出口，特别是金融服务，尚未恢复到公投前的增长轨迹，而外国投资已转向阿姆斯特丹、都柏林、法兰克福和巴黎。正如英国物理学家理查德·琼斯所指出的，到2023年底，英国人均实际国内生产总值比1955年至2008年间的增长趋势预测低约28%。脱欧并非唯一原因——长期疲弱的生产力、数十年的投资不足，以及2008年危机后多年紧缩政策的长期阴影，这些都先于公投存在——但它加剧了现有趋势，并封锁了最便捷的回归增长之路。

在公投后的大部分时期，这些成本是分散的，很容易被Dismissed as the special pleading of economists who had opposed Brexit all along（当作一直反对脱欧的经济学家的特殊诉求而不予理会）。英国相对韧性的就业状况（其中大部分是低薪、低生产力的服务业工作）使得宣称末日论者错了成为可能。他们并没有错。他们只是说得太早了。

最清晰的信号出现在英国债券市场。政府债券收益率是国家借款时支付的利率。收益率上升表明贷款方认为向该国放贷的风险在增加。这使得偿还国债成本更高，留给其他用途的资金更少。十年期英国政府债券收益率在2025年稳步攀升，延续至2026年。这主要不是工党财政决策的结果。里夫斯比她的保守党前任更加自律，她为引导经济政策而采用的财政规则也比前任财政大臣杰里米·亨特设定的更为严格。然而债券收益率持续上升。部分原因是全球性的：美联储一直迟迟不降息，这使得全球范围内借贷成本居高不下，并将其他政府债务的收益率拖高，包括英国在内。同时，期限溢价——投资者持有长期债务所要求的额外补偿——在各地也都上升了。但英国一直持续表现逊于同行。其十年期收益率现在与法国可比债券相比溢价约115个基点，与德国债券相比溢价185个基点——这意味着英国必须比其邻国为相同期限的借款支付明显更高的成本。这种利差反映的不是伦敦的公共财政状况（与巴黎和柏林相似），而是相对增长前景和减弱了的抗冲击能力。投资者看不到英国如何在不从根本上改变与欧盟的关系或实施可能挑战政治容忍极限的持续紧缩的情况下，通过增长摆脱疲弱增长和高昂借贷成本这一困境。

过去一年最引人注目的发展是公众对脱欧态度悄然但明确的转变。自2022年以来的YouGov民调数据显示，多数选民认为脱欧是一个错误。事实上，本月有56%的英国人表示离开欧盟是错误的。在工党选民中，这种转变最为显著：约五分之四支持就关税同盟问题开启谈判。关于重新加入欧盟尚无共识，但辩论已不再是关于脱欧是否兑现了其承诺。取而代之的是，辩论涉及英国应该在多大程度上重新与欧洲建立联系。如此多的选民仍然被法拉奇所吸引——他曾为脱欧助选，现在主张的不是说脱欧是个错误，而是说政治阶层未能兑现脱欧的承诺——这与其说反映了对脱欧的热情，不如说反映了对传统政党的彻底幻灭。改革党的崛起是由对移民、停滞的生活水平和失败的公共服务的愤怒所推动的，而且大量支持者同时在民调中表示离开欧盟是个错误。然而，这种情绪变化尚未转化为政策。斯塔默的团队因2019年大选而伤痕累累——当时工党支持就脱欧问题举行第二次公投，导致其在英格兰北部的“红色城墙”失去了数十个原本最安全的席位。因此，斯塔默决心避免做任何可能被塑造为背叛脱欧的事情。2024年工党竞选宣言排除了重新加入单一市场和关税同盟的可能性，只承诺与布鲁塞尔进行模糊的“重置”。执政后，该党达成了一项安全伙伴关系、一项关于农产品贸易卫生方面的温和协议、英国恢复参与伊拉斯谟学生交流计划，以及一些能源领域的技术合作。这些远远不足以满足选民的关切——他们不太关心学生交流或兽医文书工作，而是更关心高昂的生活成本、停滞的工资和已濒临崩溃的公共服务。解读同僚对斯塔默的挑战需要在这个背景下理解。前卫生大臣韦斯·斯特里廷——在决定支持伯纳姆之前一直在考虑自己的领袖竞选——走得最远，他在5月呼吁英国重新加入欧盟。伯纳姆不能如此大胆地亲欧。迈基菲尔德在2016年投票支持离开欧盟，因此他在补选中将欧洲话题排除在讨论之外。这种自律得到了回报，但他新选区的倾向将成为伯纳姆持续的紧张来源。在全国范围内，多数人可能希望与布鲁塞尔建立更紧密的联系，但直接威胁伯纳姆议席的来自疑欧派的改革党。

伯纳姆——现在几乎确定将在7月中旬被宣布为斯塔默的继任者——面临一个可以说是三重困境的局面。新首相可以实现三个目标中的任意两个：脱欧公投结果的持续合法性、通过可信的增长战略恢复债券市场的信誉，或者维持2024年将工党重新推上权力宝座的工党选举联盟。但他无法三者兼得。尊重公投结果并维持联盟团结，债券市场就会在增长令人失望、财政空间萎缩时施加其 discipline（约束）。这本质上就是斯塔默-里夫斯策略，而其失败正日益明显。追求与欧盟的关税同盟或单一市场重新调整以恢复增长，改革党将发起“脱欧背叛”运动，可能瓦解工党的多数优势。试图玩弄花招——在某些领域深化一体化，在其他领域在言辞上维护脱欧——可能导致最坏的两面结果：对市场来说太少，对脱欧派来说太多 drift（偏离）。

当今局势最类似于1976年，当时詹姆斯·卡拉汉首相的工党政府在国内货币危机中不得不向国际货币基金组织求助。那一事件被视为耻辱——而且确实是。但它也是外部约束迫使工党放弃战后凯恩斯主义通过积极需求管理维持充分就业策略的时刻。这是一个政府没有选择但被市场、通胀，最终被 IMF 所强加的转型。



当前领导层面临着相似的时刻。长期以来让英国即使表现不佳也不至于陷入经济危机的缓冲机制——低利率、规则-based贸易体系、以及欧盟成员国身份——都已被一一剥离。如今剩下的经济现实是：必须以比以往更快的速度增长，必须以高于承受能力的利率融资，必须面对一代人以来最恶劣的全球环境。很快，某种崩溃就不可避免。如果存在摆脱这一困境的出路，那在于一项宏大的交易：英国向欧盟开放其可观的军事和情报能力，以换取持久的单一市场准入。这一安排将带来足够的融合度以满足英国债券市场的需求，同时又不至于因否定脱欧而瓦解工党的政治联盟。自布莱斯和我2024年在《外交事务》上提出这一建议以来，其必要性愈发凸显。当时的担忧是欧洲过于被动、过度依赖华盛顿，无法提供值得英国出手的协议。那个欧洲已经不复存在。被特朗普的行动所震动——他抛弃乌克兰、威胁格陵兰、在北约问题上摇摆不定——欧洲大陆已开始笨拙地认真对待自身的防务。今年初，欧盟启动了一项1750亿美元的重新武装计划，首次以集体借款方式为联合防务项目融资，重建已被掏空的工业基础。脱欧现在在整个欧洲大陆被广泛视为一个警示案例。然而，未来的合作核心并非由布鲁塞尔机构建立，而是由“意愿联盟”构建，其中最重要的是由法国和英国领导的联盟。在美国援助从2024年的190多亿美元骤降至2026年的区区4亿美元之后，巴黎和伦敦支撑起了乌克兰的防御，并在4月召集51个国家规划独立行动以重新开放霍尔木兹海峡。英国已经提供了欧洲保卫乌克兰和威慑俄罗斯所需资金和人力。但因为不在单一市场内，作为欧洲重要行为体所获得的经济红利却分文未得。一项宏大交易将解决这一问题。欧洲人可能不愿奖励一个选择离开的国家，但现状对欧盟同样不利。当前的英国贡献是自愿性的、可逆的，受制于伦敦的执政者。一项具有约束力的条约将把英国军队、资金和情报长期锁定在欧洲防务中。而且英国之所以能够为此要价，正是因为欧洲大陆再也无法依赖美国。最大胆的版本——也是正确的版本——不应将安全作为贸易协议的附加品。相反，伦敦和布鲁塞尔应签署单一市场和海关协议，同时建立具有约束力的安全伙伴关系。经济协议将恢复脱欧所摧毁的大部分内容，包括商品和农产品的无摩擦贸易以及选择性进入服务业，但允许例外条款以赢得英国公众的支持：不承担采用欧元的义务，不实行人员自由流动。布鲁塞尔不会轻易在后者问题上让步，但因为迫切需要英国的防务能力，它可能愿意接受针对年轻人或技术工人的有管理的、互惠的人员流动方案，以取代无条件的自由流动。与此同时，安全协议将通过联合采购、综合规划和指挥以及对欧洲任务的保证贡献，把英国武装力量、国防工业和情报机构与欧洲集体防务紧密绑定——以条约形式确保这一伙伴关系能够经受住伦敦政治起伏。

这一切都不会轻易实现。但工党手中的牌比其红线所显示的怯懦更为强大。英国应将自己定位为欧洲防务项目中不可或缺贡献者——该项目终于开始认真对待自身——并据此要求相应回报。继续衰落、收益率上升、财政紧缩、政党缓慢的选举自杀——这是一条死路。在2017年初发表于《外交事务》的一篇文章中，我主张脱欧对英国的损害将大于对欧盟的损害，成本将不可阻挡地累积，这一变化终将迫使人们直面现实。这一预测已被证实。欧盟没有崩溃。相反，它通过一系列危机——疫情、乌克兰战争、特朗普的回归——变得更加团结，而脱欧现在在整个欧洲大陆被视为一个警示案例而非值得追随的道路。英国已深刻体会到，在一个整合的世界中，渺小并不等同于主权。伯纳姆在梅克菲尔德的决定性胜利让他有理由声称自己是唯一能让改革党望而却步的工党人物。如果他如预期般接替斯塔默成为工党领袖，他将不得不直面这一困境，面对如今已成为重要选举力量的改革党，以及疲惫、愤怒、确信政治阶层尚未坦诚面对未来取舍的选民。脱欧本质上是反抗一个已让英国大部分人口失望的经济安排。悲剧在于，它产生了一个更糟的安排。英国离开欧盟可以不承担经济后果——这始终是一个虚构，但现在这一观念已被彻底证伪。工党是否会说出置身欧盟之外的真实代价，以及英国的政治和经济体系能否在不引发更深层合法性危机的情况下接受这一真相，这些仍有待观察。上世纪70年代，卡拉汉政府最终接受了市场施加的纪律，工党为此付出了在野18年的代价。但这一代工党政治家尚未接受自身的困境，仍在试图周旋于经济和地缘政治现实已不再允许的立场。以能干管理承诺赢得权力的政党，或许仍能鼓起勇气承认前任造成的损害，并开始——尽管谨慎地——着手修复部分损害。脱欧的账单已到期，总得有人来支付。唯一的问题是，工党是按照能够继续执政的条件来支付，还是按照确保其无法执政的条件来支付。
articles.summary:
When British Prime Minister Keir Starmer led the Labour Party to a landslide victory in July 2024, he hailed the result as the end of 14 years of misrule by the Conservative Party and the beginning of a long period of stable, technocratic government. Starmer, the cautious former director of public prosecutions, drew a deliberate contrast to Conservative Prime Minister Boris Johnson’s illegal parties during the COVID-19 lockdown and the financial meltdown triggered by Johnson’s successor, Liz Truss, by promising a return to seriousness, and his Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves vowed to pair fiscal responsibility with a more active state. The exhausted electorate gave Labour a modest 33.7 percent of the vote, which translated to 411 out of 650 seats in the House of Commons because of the way votes were distributed. It was no ringing endorsement—immediately dubbed a “loveless landslide”—but it was more than enough to earn Starmer the benefit of the doubt.

Almost two years later, voters’ doubt has hardened into something closer to contempt. Labour has been trailing Nigel Farage’s insurgent Reform UK party in most national polls by almost ten points, a sharp contrast to the 2024 election, when Reform’s share of the vote was nearly 20 points below Labour’s. On June 22, his position no longer tenable, Starmer bowed to the inevitable and announced that he will step down as soon as Labour chooses his successor. His all but inevitable successor is Andy Burnham, the popular former mayor of Greater Manchester who joined Parliament after winning a by-election in Makerfield on June 18 with nearly 55 percent of the vote.

Most explanations for Labour’s troubles focus on tactical errors: Starmer’s communication failures, Reeves’ overly strict fiscal rules, a cut in 2024 to pensioners’ winter fuel payments, and a tax on family farms imposed in 2025. All are real, but they are symptoms, not causes. The deeper story is that Brexit, the defining political event of modern British history, is finally presenting its bill, and Labour happens to be in office as it arrives. For a decade, the United Kingdom managed to defer a full reckoning with its vote to leave the European Union in June 2016. Starmer’s strategy depended on neutralizing Brexit as a live political issue, defusing it so that it could neither rally his opponents nor split his own coalition. This meant ruling out a customs union or membership in the single market and dressing up modest regulatory cooperation with the EU as the limit of his ambition.

Electorally, that strategy worked. It cost nothing in the short term, and in the comparatively forgiving conditions of 2024, the British economy could underperform its peers but avoid an acute crisis. That environment no longer exists. U.S. President Donald Trump’s return to the White House, his weaponization of tariffs, and the breakdown of the rules-based trading order have exposed a British economy with no shock absorbers left. The EU, for all its dysfunctions, has a huge internal market and the scale to negotiate with the great powers as more than a supplicant. The United Kingdom does not.

The best way now for the United Kingdom to weather the global storm—and the best way for Labour to keep its coalition from falling apart—is to pursue closer partnership with the EU. There is reason to think a grand bargain is possible. The British economy needs access to European markets, and Europe needs Britain’s military power. What is needed is a final, elusive ingredient: political will.

WHAT HAS GONE

To understand why the United Kingdom is suffering more than its continental neighbors from recent economic shocks, it helps to recall what EU membership provided. The single market gave British firms barrier-free access to 450 million consumers and made the United Kingdom a magnet for foreign investment seeking a foothold in Europe. The customs union spared British exports the rules-of-origin checks, sanitary inspections, and paperwork that now burden shipments across the English Channel. Rules that allowed EU firms to operate anywhere in the bloc—a privilege known as passporting—made the City of London the continent’s de facto financial center, and coordinated regulation gave the United Kingdom a seat at the table when European standards were written. None of this required the United Kingdom to give up its currency, central bank, fiscal policy, or army. The opt-outs British Prime Minister John Major negotiated at Maastricht in the early 1990s gave the country most of the upside of integration with little of the supranational constraint. This was a deal the rest of Europe tolerated because the United Kingdom brought military weight, financial expertise, and a counterweight to Franco-German dominance.

The decision to leave that arrangement was, as Mark Blyth and I argued in Foreign Affairs two years ago, a colossal economic own goal. The introduction of nontariff barriers raised the cost of trading with Europe by an amount the British government–funded Office for Budget Responsibility now puts at roughly four percent of long-run GDP. This means a permanent reduction in national income to the order of $132 billion a year, just under $4,650 for the average household. British exports of services to the EU, financial services above all, have not recovered their pre-referendum trajectory, and foreign investment has shifted toward Amsterdam, Dublin, Frankfurt, and Paris. As the British physicist Richard Jones has noted, by the end of 2023, British real per capita GDP was roughly 28 percent below what growth trends between 1955 and 2008 predicted. Brexit is not the sole cause—chronically weak productivity, years of underinvestment, and the long shadow of the 2008 crisis that was followed by years of austerity all predate the referendum—but it worsened existing trends and foreclosed the easiest routes back to growth. For most of the post-referendum period, these costs were diffuse and easy to dismiss as the special pleading of economists who had opposed Brexit all along. Resilient British employment (much of it low-paid, low-productivity service work) made it possible to claim the doom-mongers had been wrong. They had not been wrong. They had just been early.

The clearest signal is in the British bond market. A government bond yield is the interest rate that a country pays to borrow. A rising yield signals that lenders see the risk of lending to that country as increasing. It makes the servicing of the national debt more expensive, leaving less money for everything else. The yield on ten-year British government debt has climbed steadily through 2025 and into 2026. This is not primarily the result of Labour’s fiscal decisions. Reeves has been more disciplined than her Conservative predecessors, and the fiscal rules she adopted to guide her economic policy are tighter than those set by the previous Chancellor of the Exchequer, Jeremy Hunt. Yet bond yields keep rising. Part of the explanation is global: the U.S. Federal Reserve has been slow to cut interest rates, which keeps borrowing costs elevated worldwide and drags up yields on other governments’ debt, the United Kingdom’s included. Meanwhile, term premia—the additional compensation investors demand for holding longer-dated debt—have also risen everywhere.

But the United Kingdom has consistently underperformed its peers. Its ten-year yields now trade at a premium of roughly 115 basis points over comparable French bonds and 185 over German ones—meaning the United Kingdom must pay markedly more than its neighbors to borrow for the same length of time. This spread reflects not the state of London’s public finances, which are similar to those of Paris and Berlin, but relative growth prospects and a diminished capacity to absorb shocks. Investors cannot see how the United Kingdom grows out of the trap of weak growth and expensive borrowing without either a fundamental change in its relationship with the EU or a sustained austerity that would test the limits of political tolerance.

SECOND THOUGHTS

The most striking development of the past year has been the quiet but unmistakable shift in public opinion on Brexit. Polling by YouGov since 2022 has shown a majority of the electorate views Brexit as a mistake. Indeed, this month, 56 percent of Britons said it was wrong to leave. Among Labour voters, the shift is starkest: roughly four in five support opening negotiations on a customs union. There is no consensus for rejoining the EU, but the debate is no longer about whether Brexit delivered on its promises. Instead, the debate concerns how closely the United Kingdom should reconnect with Europe. That so many of these voters are nonetheless drawn to Farage—who campaigned for Brexit and now argues not that it was a mistake but that the political class failed to deliver on it—says less about enthusiasm for Brexit than about a complete disillusionment with the established parties. Reform’s rise is powered by anger over immigration, stagnant living standards, and failing public services, and a large number of the people who back it tell pollsters in the same breath that leaving the EU was a mistake.

This change in sentiment has not yet translated into policy, however. Starmer’s team was scarred by the 2019 election in which Labour’s support for a second referendum on leaving the EUcost it scores of seats in northern England that had previously been among the party’s most secure (its so-called “Red Wall”). As a consequence, Starmer was determined to avoid doing anything that could be cast as a betrayal of Brexit. The 2024 Labour manifesto ruled out rejoining the single market and the customs union and promised only a vague “reset” with Brussels. In office, the party has produced a security partnership, a modest agreement related to sanitation in the trade of agricultural products, the United Kingdom’s resuming its participation in the Erasmus scheme for students, and some technical cooperation on energy. This is hardly enough to satisfy voters who care less about student exchanges or veterinary paperwork than about the high cost of living, flat wages, and public services stretched to the breaking point.

The challenge to Starmer by fellow Labour politicians must be read in this context. Former Health Secretary Wes Streeting—who had been considering his own leadership bid before deciding to support Burnham—has gone furthest, calling in May for the United Kingdom to rejoin the European Union. Burnham cannot be as boldly pro-European. Makerfield voted to leave the EU in 2016, and accordingly, he kept discussion of Europe out of the by-election. The discipline paid off, but the leanings of his new constituency will remain a source of tension for Burnham. Nationally, a majority may want closer ties with Brussels, but the immediate threat to Burnham’s seat comes from the Euroskeptic Reform.

TRIPLE TROUBLE

Burnham, who is now overwhelmingly likely to be crowned Starmer’s successor in mid-July, faces what is best described as a trilemma. The new prime minister can have any two of three objectives: continued legitimacy of the Brexit settlement, restored credibility with bond markets through a plausible growth strategy, or unity of the Labour electoral coalition that returned the party to power in 2024. But he cannot have all three. Honor the referendum and keep the coalition together, and the bond markets impose their discipline as growth disappoints, and fiscal headroom shrinks. This, in essence, has been the Starmer-Reeves strategy, and its failure is increasingly evident. Pursue a customs union or single-market realignment with the EU to restore growth, and Reform will run a “Brexit betrayal” campaign that could fracture Labour’s majority. Try to finesse it—deeper integration in some sectors, rhetorical defense of Brexit in others—and the worst of both worlds could result: too little to satisfy markets and too much drift to pacify Brexiteers.

The situation today most closely resembles that of 1976, when Prime Minister James Callaghan’s Labour government went cap in hand to the International Monetary Fund amid a currency crisis. That episode is remembered as a humiliation—and it was. But it was also the moment that external discipline forced Labour to abandon the postwar Keynesian strategy of sustaining full employment through active demand management. This was a transition the government did not choose but had imposed on it by markets, inflation, and ultimately, the IMF. The current leadership faces a similar moment. The shock absorbers that allowed the United Kingdom to underperform without sliding into an economic crisis—low global interest rates, the rules-based trading order, and EU membership itself—have all been stripped away. What remains is an economy that must grow faster than it has been, that needs to be financed at rates higher than it can afford, and that faces the most hostile global environment in a generation. Before long, something will have to give.

If there is an exit from the trilemma, it lies in a grand bargain whereby the United Kingdom offers the EU access to its substantial military and intelligence capabilities in return for durable access to the single market. Such an arrangement would enable enough integration to satisfy the British bond markets without the repudiation of Brexit that would shatter Labour’s coalition. The case for doing so has only sharpened since Blyth and I proposed it in Foreign Affairs in 2024. The worry then was that Europe was too passive and dependent on Washington to make a deal worth Britain’s while. That Europe is gone. Jolted by Trump’s actions—his abandonment of Ukraine, his threats to Greenland, and his wavering on NATO—the continent has begun, haltingly, to take its own defense seriously. Early this year, the EU began disbursing a $175 billion rearmament program, borrowing collectively for the first time in its history to finance joint defense projects and rebuild a hollowed-out industrial base.

Brexit is now widely understood across the continent as a cautionary tale.

The core of future cooperation is being built, however, not by Brussels institutions but by “coalitions of the willing,” the most important of which is led by France and the United Kingdom. Paris and London anchored Ukraine’s defense after U.S. aid collapsed from more than $19 billion in 2024 to a token $400 million in 2026, and in April they convened 51 countries to plan an independent mission to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. The United Kingdom already supplies the money and manpower that Europe needs to defend Ukraine and deter Russia. But, because it is not in the single market, it collects none of the economic dividend from being a serious European actor. A grand bargain would fix that. Europeans might balk at rewarding a country that chose to leave, but the status quo no longer serves the EU well. Current British contributions are voluntary and reversible, hostage to whoever governs in London. A binding treaty would lock British forces, money, and intelligence into Europe’s defense for the long term. And the United Kingdom is in a position to charge for those contributions precisely because the continent can no longer count on the United States.

The boldest version, and the right one, would not treat security as a sweetener to a trade deal. Instead, London and Brussels should sign a single-market and customs agreement as well as a binding security partnership. The economic deal would restore most of what Brexit destroyed, including friction-free trade in goods and agriculture and selective access in services, but allow opt-outs to make it acceptable to the British public: no obligation to adopt the euro and no free movement of people. Brussels would not concede on the latter lightly, but because it urgently needs British defense capabilities, it may be willing to accept managed, reciprocal labor-mobility schemes for the young or for skilled workers in place of unconditional free movement. The security deal, meanwhile, would tie the British armed forces, defense industry, and intelligence services into Europe’s collective defense—through joint procurement, integrated planning and command, and guaranteed contributions to European missions—by treaty, ensuring that the partnership endures through political ups and downs in London.

None of this will come easily. But Labour’s hand is stronger than the timidity of its red lines suggests. The United Kingdom should present itself not as a supplicant but as an indispensable contributor to a European security project finally getting serious, and ask to be paid accordingly. The alternative of continued decline, rising yields, fiscal contraction, and the slow electoral suicide of the party is a route that leads nowhere.

THE END OF THE ROAD

In an essay published in Foreign Affairs in early 2017, I argued that Brexit would damage the United Kingdom more than the European Union, the costs would build inexorably, and the change would eventually force a reckoning. That prediction has been borne out. The EU has not collapsed. Instead, it has grown more cohesive through a series of crises—the pandemic, the war in Ukraine, the return of Trump—and Brexit is now widely understood across the continent as a cautionary tale rather than a path to follow. The United Kingdom has learned the hard way that smallness in an integrated world is not the same as sovereignty.

Burnham’s commanding win in Makerfield lets him claim, with some justification, to be the one Labour figure who can keep Reform at bay. If, as seems likely, he is elected as Starmer’s successor, he will have to confront the trilemma head on, facing a Reform party that is now a serious electoral force and an electorate that is exhausted, angry, and convinced that the political class has not been honest about the tradeoffs ahead.

Brexit was, at its core, a revolt against an economic settlement that had failed large parts of the British population. The tragedy is that it produced an even worse settlement. It was always a fiction that the United Kingdom could leave the EU without incurring economic consequences, but now the idea has been disproved conclusively. It remains to be seen whether Labour will tell the truth about the cost of staying outside the EU, and whether the country’s political and economic system can absorb that truth without a deeper crisis of legitimacy. In the 1970s, the Callaghan government eventually accepted the discipline that markets impose, and Labour paid for it by spending 18 years out of government. But this generation of Labour politicians has not yet accepted its own predicament and is still trying to finesse a position that economic and geopolitical reality no longer permits.

The party that won power on a promise of competent management may yet be able to summon the courage to acknowledge the damage that its predecessors did and to begin, however cautiously, to undo some of it. Brexit’s bill has come due, and someone will have to pay it. The only question is whether Labour does so on terms that allow it to govern—or on terms that ensure it does not.

articles.title: Losing the War of the Future

在最近对伊朗的军事行动中，美国运用传统空中力量主导了天空。美国军方轰炸伊朗目标，实施了超过13000次打击。然而，这种实力和毁灭性火力并未阻止伊朗的反击。在2月28日开始、4月8日结束的为期39天的冲突中，伊朗向该地区国家发射了超过2200枚导弹和4400架无人机。至少8架美国军机被伊朗攻击摧毁或损坏。多部美国雷达被击中，7名美国军人阵亡。截至本文撰写之时，伊朗政权依然存在，并继续控制着霍尔木兹海峡。美国在这场战争中未能实现其目标，尽管从各项指标来看，美国的实力远胜于伊朗。美国军方长期以来依赖的技术优势正在减弱。与过去不同，美国曾在大规模领先隐形技术和精确制导武器领域占据优势，但在当前这个由无人机和人工智能定义战争形态的时代，美国将无法再获得技术优势。与伊朗的冲突是美国首次体验这种新型战争时代。新兴技术正在拉平华盛顿与其对手之间的差距。廉价无人机技术的扩散和人工智能能力的普及，使较小的国家和非国家行为体也有了超越自身分量的机会。这些对手现在可以打击美国后方基地，造成人员伤亡并损坏昂贵的美国军机。伊朗用导弹攻击海湾地区的美国基地，摧毁了一架E-3“哨兵”预警机。这一损失甚至超过了这架价值3亿美元飞机的成本，因为美国E-3机队现在仅剩15架，而替代项目还需数年才能实现。伊朗导弹还击中了5架KC-135“同温层油船”加油机以及多部美国地面雷达。无人机不仅改变了战争动态，还改变了战争经济学。在海湾地区和其他地方，低成本空中和海上无人机以及导弹可以摧毁价值高得多的资产。乌克兰使用神风特攻队无人艇和反舰导弹，在两年战争中重创俄罗斯黑海舰队，击沉13艘舰船，击伤数十艘。一艘价值30万美元的无人艇可以瘫痪一艘价值数亿美元的海军战舰。美国仍然拥有世界上最强大的军队，但它尚未为这种现实所定义的新型战争时代做好准备。美国需要生产更多低成本无人机和拦截导弹，需要更好地适应人工智能竞争的迫切需求。正如军队没有飞机就无法集结空中力量、没有舰船就无法控制海洋一样，没有数据的支撑、计算能力的获取以及对人工智能模型的最佳利用，美国就无法在人工智能时代获胜。为了在战场上保持优势，美国军方必须找到有效整合这些新技术的方法。这需要克服武装部队内部的文化和官僚障碍，与私营部门建立更紧密的关系，并找到评估军事实力的新方法。但如果美国军方不以此方式进行调整，它将越来越多地发现自己在战场上与对手势均力敌。经过数十年的技术优势保障的主导地位，美国将因其领先地位危险地滑落而衰落。美国长期以来一直依靠技术创新来获取对对手的优势。冷战初期，美国国防规划者依赖核武器来抵消苏联在欧洲的军事数量优势。1970年代，美国在军事规划中引入了信息革命，半导体、计算机网络和卫星的进步使其在隐形系统、精确制导武器和GPS方面取得了领先地位。这些技术在1990-91年的海湾战争中证明了其价值，美国系统性地瓦解了伊拉克军队。在2003年伊拉克入侵中，其效果更加令人印象深刻，美国军队仅用三周就攻占了巴格达。2014年，五角大楼启动了“第三次抵消”战略，试图利用机器人和人工智能来弥补中国和俄罗斯军队的数量优势。这一战略推动美国军方利用商业领域出现的人工智能技术，并使美国官员相信他们可以巩固对对手的持久技术优势。但这一次，这样的战略将不起作用。美国在新兴技术方面不再具有明显优势，也不会获得这样的优势。以无人驾驶车辆为例。廉价无人机在世界范围内广泛可得，美国将无法阻止竞争对手大量部署它们。伊朗近年来已成为廉价无人机的主要生产国，已向俄罗斯提供了数千架无人机用于乌克兰战争。基于伊朗的设计，俄罗斯已经生产了数万多架无人机。从理论上讲，美国应该能够大量生产这些武器。低成本无人机不依赖任何特殊技术。但实际上，美国军方在部署任何数量可观的廉价无人机方面一直举步维艰。乌克兰每年生产400万架无人机，而美国陆军仅采购50000架。拜登和特朗普两届政府的五角大楼领导人都将生产低成本无人机作为优先事项，但结构性障碍阻碍了这一进程。小型军用无人机依赖最初为商业爱好者市场开发的技术，该市场由中国大疆公司主导。美国军方合理地不想依赖其主要竞争对手的军事硬件，因此最终购买的是价格更高的美国制造无人机（这些无人机仍然经常使用中国零部件）。算力类似于工业时代的制造业产能。更具破坏性的是，美国根本不擅长廉价、快速或大规模地生产任何东西。几十年来，美国国防生产一直在成本曲线上稳步攀升，走向越来越“精美”的国防平台——这是军事术语，指的是先进、昂贵、低产量的武器。相比之下，无人机已经使军事格局向低成本、可消耗（或一次性）的武器倾斜，这些武器可以大规模生产。美国一直适应缓慢。国防部2023年的“复制者”计划旨在快速部署数千个低成本自主系统，但只产生了数百个。现任五角大楼领导层已宣布扩大低成本无人机生产的计划，承诺投入超过10亿美元，到2027年生产340000架无人机。陆军设定了更为雄心勃勃的目标，即到2028年至少生产100万架无人机。为实现这些目标，军方需要提供持续和大量的资金来建设一个尚不存在大规模的小型无人机工业基础。但无人机技术并未停滞不前。很快，这些飞行器将能够以更大的自主性运行，并与其它机器更紧密地协调作战。今天的大多数无人机是由人远程驾驶的，或使用简单的自动化，例如沿着指定航点飞行，或者在失去与人类飞行员之间的通信连接时返回基地。乌克兰已成为更复杂自主功能的试验场。例如，许多乌克兰无人机具有自主末端制导功能，允许无人驾驶飞机在敌方干扰切断机器与人类飞行员之间的通信链路时，自主导航数百米到达目标。乌克兰还在生产远程攻击无人机，可以飞行600英里，并通过将机载摄像头图像与预加载的卫星图像匹配来自主导航，不依赖GPS。这些创新将在乌克兰以外被广泛采用。更多国家和非国家行为体很快将拥有类似的无人机，即使对手能够阻止通信并阻止无人机访问GPS，也能击中目标。无人机将配备越来越复杂的自主制导系统，允许它们自主搜索广阔区域、识别和攻击目标。这些进步将以深刻的方式改变战争。今天简单的无人机将成为明天的智能蜂群：数千架无人机实时响应战场条件的变化。蜂群将被用来追踪移动目标，进行同时攻击以压倒防御系统，建立能够抵御敌方干扰、破坏或攻击的通信和后勤网络。自主机器人蜂群将能够以人类飞行员永远无法复制的速度、协调性和动态性行动。充分利用无人机蜂群将需要从根本上重新思考军事指挥与控制、组织结构，以及人类指挥官如何在战场上指挥军事力量。军事操作员不会直接驾驶无人机。他们将指挥由数百或数千架无人机组成的整个蜂群，无人机本身自主协调其行为。各国军队将需要弄清楚应该给蜂群什么类型的指令，自主无人机之间应该如何协调。这将需要对军事传统指挥模式进行相当大的改变，用更分散的结构取代等级结构。无人机已经在以美国尚未应对的方式改变战场动态。例如在乌克兰战争中，持续在头顶的无人机使双方都难以集中兵力。无人机现在已成为俄罗斯伤亡的主要原因，取代了火炮。伊朗战争表明，无人机已使远离前线的基地变得脆弱。美国军方将不得不适应这一新现实，在伪装、诱饵和其他隐藏和分散部队以降低风险的方法上投入更多资金。美国还需要更有效的方式来防御对手可以发射的大量导弹和廉价无人机。导弹防御自海湾战争以来已经取得了长足进步，当时美国“爱国者”导弹系统在拦截伊拉克瞄准以色列的飞毛腿导弹方面几乎完全无效。但进攻性导弹技术也在发展，无人机威胁也在激增。最终结果是，尽管美国在加速前进，但却失去了阵地。今天的导弹防御是有效的，但成本高昂。美国、以色列和海湾国家自2月底以来击落了1700枚伊朗弹道导弹和无人机，但成本交换比严重有利于伊朗。用价值400万美元的“爱国者”导弹拦截一架价值35000美元（或者根据一些最新估计仅7000美元）的“沙赫德”无人机，这永远只能是一场得不偿失的胜利。华盛顿眼睁睁看着资产负债表上的损失不断增加。美国军方没有足够的导弹拦截器，对伊朗的战争已经严重耗尽了美国的库存。自战争开始以来，美国已经消耗了大约一半的“爱国者”导弹以及50%到80%的“萨德”拦截导弹。特朗普政府正在采取措施扩大产能，但弥补这些损失需要数年时间。这些库存的耗尽将使美国在中东、亚洲和欧洲的军队都处于脆弱状态。与低成本无人机一样，五角大楼正在采取措施开发和扩大低成本拦截导弹的生产规模。美国“土狼”无人机拦截器每架成本约125000美元，而“梅罗普斯”无人机拦截器每架成本约15000美元，这比价值数百万美元的导弹有了重大改进。华盛顿将需要扩大生产这些更便宜的拦截器，以跟上日益增长的威胁。人工智能将给战争带来更加全面的变革。尽管美国拥有世界上领先的人工智能公司，但该领域的进步将进一步加速美国军事技术优势的侵蚀。华盛顿被所谓的美中“人工智能竞赛”所困扰，但现实基本上是技术对等。中国的人工智能模型仅领先于美国领先模型几个月。中国的深度求索、月之暗面和 MiniMax 等公司有效地搭便车利用了美国的开放研究。开源模型的兴起意味着任何人都可以免费下载最先进的人工智能系统，并在自己的服务器上运行。这意味着美国在人工智能方面的任何优势都可能在数月内被复制。美国在人工智能领域不再拥有持久的技术优势，而且很可能永远无法获得持久优势。在某种程度上，这反映了美国自身的开放性和创新生态系统的实力。但这也意味着美国无法像过去那样依赖技术优势来维持军事主导地位。中国已经部署了大量先进武器，包括高超音速导弹和第五代隐形战斗机。伊朗已经拥有大量无人机和导弹能力。美国必须接受它将不得不在一个技术差距已大大缩小的世界中运作。这对美国的国防战略和军费开支都有深远影响。美国需要重新思考其军事力量的结构和部署方式。它需要更多的分散式基地、更强的分布式能力，以及更有效的应对大量无人机和导弹攻击的方法。这还需要与盟国更紧密地合作，因为集体防御比任何单一国家单独防御都更有效。人工智能战争将要求重新思考训练和条令。人类操作员将需要学习与自主系统合作，而不是简单地控制它们。军事教育将需要强调批判性思维、适应性和与机器协作的能力。决策将越来越多地涉及人类和人工智能系统之间的伙伴关系，而不是人类单独做出所有决定。这些变化都不会容易。但它们对于美国保持其军事优势是必要的。忽视这些趋势将是危险的。在一个无人机和人工智能正在重塑战争的世界里，自满将是失败的秘诀。美国必须拥抱这些新技术，同时也要认识到它们的局限性。无人机和人工智能是工具，就像所有工具一样，它们可以以好的方式或坏的方式使用。关键是学会有效地使用它们，同时保持对它们被滥用的警惕。未来的战争将不同于过去的任何战争。无人机蜂群、人工智能辅助决策和自主武器系统将创造一个全新的战争领域。美国必须准备好在这一新领域中作战，否则就有被甩在后面的风险。这不仅仅是关于技术，而是关于适应能力、学习速度和战略思维。美国在历史上一直是一个适应能力强的国家。它从独立战争中吸取了教训，在工业时代蓬勃发展，并在信息革命中处于领先地位。现在，它必须在人工智能时代做同样的事情。这需要领导力、资源和集体决心。但美国已经一次又一次地证明了它可以做到这一点。问题不是美国能否适应，而是它将以多快的速度适应。伊朗战争已经表明，时间不站在美国一边。竞争对手正在快速发展廉价无人机能力。美国必须加快步伐，否则就有在战场上发现自己与对手势均力敌的风险——这是美国几十年来从未遇到过的情况。



模型，并以此训练自己的模型，成本却只是原成本的一小部分。Anthropic、OpenAI和谷歌都已发现并报告了外国竞争对手大规模提取美国模型信息的活动，这些行为违反了相关模型的服务条款。中国公司受美国出口管制的限制，无法获得先进的人工智能芯片，因此通过复制美国公司——这些公司拥有最强大、最先进的芯片——所取得的成果来弥补这一差距。这种技术被称为“对抗蒸馏”，实际上抵消了美国在最尖端人工智能能力方面的优势。另一个美国直到最近仍保持优势领域是利用人工智能改造情报分析和作战规划。大语言模型已被整合到帕兰提尔公司的“梅文智能系统”中，该系统将来自多个来源的情报汇集到单一界面上，供分析人员评估战场态势。人工智能使情报分析人员和规划人员能够综合大量数据并规划打击行动。据报道，以色列军方使用机器学习系统处理数据并为加沙打击行动推荐目标，但美军针对伊朗的行动很可能是大语言模型在战场上的首次重大应用。在伊朗，美军战机经常在飞行途中被重新导向新目标，美军利用人工智能在瞬息万变的战场环境中优先排序目标并构建打击方案。美国的技术主导地位正在衰落。但再过几个月，中国军队就能获得具有相同能力的人工智能模型。事实上，地球上的每个国家和非国家行为体都将可以使用这类工具；毕竟，人工智能并非特定政府的保密技术，而是商业部门的成果，这类创新在全球传播得相当迅速。尽管领先的美国公司愿意与美国军方合作，但人工智能技术的传播速度超过了军方合理整合和采用它的速度，更不用说利用它来改造作战行动了。确实，对各国军队来说，更重要的不是哪个国家首先开发出新的人工智能工具或能力，而是哪支军队能够首先采用它。在颠覆性技术变革时期，决定军队相对成功的是它如何运用新技术。例如在二十世纪初，所有当时的主要军事大国都可以获得坦克、潜艇和飞机等新式武器。真正的挑战在于弄清楚如何最佳地使用它们。在第一次世界大战和第二次世界大战之间的时期，各国军队试验新技术，发明新的组织结构、条令和训练方法以驾驭这些武器。英国是第一个在航空母舰方面进行创新的国家，但在第二次世界大战前夕落在了日本和美国后面。英国的飞机技术是最先进的，但英国军方内部的文化和官僚障碍——比如错误地决定将海军航空的责任交给皇家空军而不是海军——减缓了技术的采用。这很重要，因为战场上起决定作用的不是最先进的设备和系统，而是方法。毕竟，大多数战争是在技术大致对等的对手之间进行的。在对1956年至1992年间陆地战争的研究中，学者斯蒂芬·比德尔发现，敌对双方在军事技术上的时间差距平均不到三年。限制中国的算力对于在人工智能采用方面领先北京至关重要，即使中国能够获得具有相同能力的人工智能模型。算力对于大规模部署人工智能至关重要。使用最先进的人工智能模型需要大量能源和算力，科技公司正投入数千亿美元建设大型数据中心以满足人工智能需求。如今，算力大致相当于工业时代的制造业产能。正如一个国家的制造业产能决定了其经济增长和军事实力一样， aggregate“算力”将决定一个国家的人工智能实力——进而决定其整体实力。美国减缓中国人工智能进展的最有力工具是出口管制，防止中国企业采购先进芯片和半导体制造设备。芯片对于训练和使用最先进的人工智能模型至关重要，美国公司在芯片生产供应链中占据关键瓶颈地位。在第一届特朗普政府和拜登政府时期，美国政府稳步加强对先进人工智能芯片和芯片制造设备对华出口的管制。但2026年1月，特朗普政府改变了做法，批准向中国出售英伟达的H200芯片。截至2026年4月，这些芯片尚未转移给中国，尽管商务部已为有限数量发放了许可证，英伟达也已收到中国客户的订单。鉴于人工智能开发芯片供应的整体限制以及美国国内需求的激增，每一块出售给中国的芯片都意味着华盛顿的损失和北京的收获。特朗普政府应该恢复对先进人工智能芯片对华销售的禁令，而不是将美国的领先地位拱手让给战略竞争对手。特朗普政府还应与日本和荷兰合作，收紧对华芯片制造设备的出口管制。先进芯片制造厂依赖于来自日本、荷兰和美国的设备。中国正拼命试图增加国内半导体制造产能，以减少对外国芯片的依赖。但如果没有关键的芯片制造设备，中国将无法生产前沿芯片。第一届特朗普政府向荷兰施加了巨大压力，要求其停止向中国销售极紫外光刻设备——这种机器是制造最先进芯片所必需的。然而，中国继续通过使用不受限制的较旧的深紫外浸没式光刻技术取得进展。当然，试图限制中国获得硬件——如芯片和芯片制造设备——对于限制其从对抗蒸馏中获益作用甚微。美国政府还应与人工智能公司合作，打击提取美国模型能力的外国竞争对手。国会应通过立法，在人工智能公司相互分享有关对抗蒸馏的信息时保护它们免受反垄断责任，类似于现有应对网络威胁的立法。美国人工智能公司之间更好的合作可以通过分享最佳实践和威胁信息来改善对抗蒸馏的防御。华盛顿应该制裁涉及非法提取美国公司人工智能模型能力的中国实体。制裁特定的中国公司将禁止美国公司与其合作，在最极端的情况下，将切断违规中国公司与国际金融体系的联系。在某些情况下，人工智能实验室本身可能希望将某些最先进的人工智能能力保留不公开，这可以减缓扩散。OpenAI和Anthropic在推迟发布最新模型时采取了这种方法，比如Anthropic的Mythos模型，因为他们担心不良行为者可能将其用于网络攻击。Anthropic与几家领先科技公司合作开展了“玻璃幕墙项目”，利用Anthropic的人工智能模型在更危险的能力扩散之前发现并修补网络安全漏洞。OpenAI创建了一个“可信访问”计划，允许数千名经过验证的网络安全专家访问OpenAI的工具用于网络防御。这些方法可以为网络安全专业人员提供抢先一步应对即将到来的危险人工智能能力的机会，但时间紧迫。截至2025年10月，人工智能研究组织Epoch AI评估，最强大的开源权重模型——即任何人都可以下载的模型——与前沿模型的差距仅为三个月。限制发布将通过使对抗蒸馏更具挑战性来减缓扩散，但这不会是永久的解决方案。Anthropic联合创始人杰克·克拉克在2026年4月估计，今天所谓的前沿人工智能网络能力的水平将在12至18个月内被广泛获取并开源。战场上起决定作用的不是设备，而是方法。华盛顿无法阻止人工智能能力的扩散，但它仍然可以获得一点优势。将三个月的领先优势扩大到18个月，为网络安全专家和美国军方争取更多时间来采用最新的人工智能技术。从这个意义上说，正确的技术方法不会给美国带来持久的优势，但会在一场持续的竞赛中为华盛顿提供小小的领先。美国需要利用这段时间进行创新、试验人工智能，并调整自己的组织和条令，以最大限度地利用最新技术。这将需要一种思维方式的转变，从美国军方在和平时期通常采用的缓慢、审慎的方法，转向基于快速迭代和适应的战时方法。美国军方在伊拉克和阿富汗战争中迅速修订了做法，快速部署设备并修改战术以应对简易爆炸装置的威胁并使用无人机监视叛乱分子。传统的官僚式五角大楼流程——为军事系统建立需求、预算成本和采购技术——将无法跟上人工智能的发展步伐并保持对对手的领先。乌克兰受到生存危机的激励，已将产量扩大到每年四百万架无人机。以乌克兰国内生产总值的140倍，美国应该能够接近这个数字。尽管五角大楼花了数年时间才在装甲车辆方面投入足够资金以认真应对伊拉克和阿富汗路边炸弹的威胁，但一旦2007年国防部长罗伯特·盖茨将其作为优先事项，军方在约一年半内就部署了一万辆装甲车辆。幸运的是，五角大楼现任领导层愿意打破常规。国防部已在其保密和非保密网络上部署了大语言模型，让国防机构内部的三百万军事和文职用户可以使用人工智能模型。五角大楼领导层还在扩展跨网络可用模型的数量，让员工可以使用多样化的人工智能平台。初步迹象是积极的。国防部报告称已有一百多万用户使用了人工智能模型。但该部将需要做更多工作来为采用创造正确的官僚和文化激励措施。这包括给予员工自由来试验人工智能并接受失败和错误。该部的一个人工智能战略于1月发布，强调了速度的重要性。为了帮助消除繁文缛节，该战略设立了一个每月一次的“障碍清除委员会”，以豁免可能阻碍人工智能采用的非立法限制。为了允许更大的数据访问，该战略指示将数据分享给授权用户，任何拒绝数据请求的决定必须在七天内说明理由。这些都是加快五角大楼步伐的受欢迎举措。但仅靠速度是不够的。充分利用新技术优势的一些最大障碍是文化方面的。技术进步需要新的作战方式，而这些东西有时会挑战军队内部根深蒂固的习惯和深厚的身份认同。美国海军在十九世纪抵制了从帆船到蒸汽机的转型，甚至在内战后倒退了一步。美军内部关于如何最有效地使用坦克的争论贯穿了整个第二次世界大战。直到1943年，陆军地面部队指挥官莱斯利·麦克奈尔中将还向陆军参谋长乔治·马歇尔将军写了一份备忘录，认为德国三年前对法国的闪电战是一种异常，坦克的适当角色是支援步兵，而不是独自领导装甲突击。今天的军种同样保守。每个军种的文化和对空中力量的看法塑造了它采用无人机的方式。陆军是第一个拥抱更自动化飞行控制的军种，包括起飞和着陆，并使用士兵作为无人机操作员。



空军抵制这些创新，因为它们挑战了空军将无人机操控员视为“飞行员”的观念。然而，空军在从美国本土基地操控无人机方面具有创新性，而陆军则选择将无人机操作员向前部署到伊拉克和阿富汗，这是一种效率低得多的兵力使用方式。将无人机操作员集中在本土基地使他们能够持续操控无人机，而陆军在伊拉克和阿富汗战争期间向前部署无人机操作员的政策意味着大约三分之二的陆军无人机操作员在部署间隙期间身在国内，无法执行飞行任务。但在陆军看来，士兵不应该远程上班参加战争。

海军对无人系统和机器人系统的热情差异很大。海军潜艇部队已在很大程度上接受了水下无人航行器，这些航行器是潜艇的补充，而非替代品。然而，在海军航空领域，航母甲板空间有限。每增加一架无人机到航母甲板上，就意味着取代了一架传统的有人驾驶战斗机。尽管隐形作战无人机可以大幅扩展航母的作战范围，但海军将航母上的无人机降级为运输燃油的加油机，用于支援而非取代有人驾驶战斗机。为了保住飞行员的岗位，海军选择牺牲航母的作战范围和打击能力。

人工智能对军队自我形象的挑战甚至比无人机更大。人工智能提出了关于人类和机器角色的根本性问题。关于人工智能抢走全社会工作岗位的担忧同样会在军队中上演，因为军人的身份认同与执行的任务紧密相连——这种联系如此强烈，以至于即使技术早已使某项任务过时，这种身份认同仍然存在。海军人员仍然被称为“水兵”，尽管他们不再爬桅杆、升降帆或操控索具。陆军仍然有士兵自认为“骑兵”，尽管他们不再骑马。这些身份作为历史遗迹延续下来，即使军人的工作发生了变化——随着人工智能改变武装部队，同样的情况也可能发生。但从蒸汽动力船到坦克再到无人机的军事技术采用历史表明，身份和文化可能是阻止军队释放新技术真正利益的强大力量。

美国的另一个重要力量是确保该国军事技术领先地位：私营部门。采用有效的人工智能需要与更广泛的行业、开发人工智能的公司以及第三方评估机构建立深度合作，这些评估机构是人工智能能力和局限性方面的专家。为此，五角大楼领导层需要修复与硅谷近年来变得紧张的关系——与Anthropic在国防部合同条款上的分歧引发了关系恶化——国防部坚持要求对其技术进行“任何合法使用”的无限制访问，而Anthropic希望对其技术用于国内大规模监控和为完全自主武器提供动力的潜在用途设置防护栏。

这关系到不仅仅是军方与一家公司的关系。公开争端引发了人工智能工程师的强烈反对，他们现在越来越反对与军方合作。超过1000名Google和OpenAI员工签署了一封公开信，敦促他们的公司“团结起来，继续拒绝国防部的当前要求”。2026年4月，超过600名Google员工签署了一封公开信，敦促该公司不允许其人工智能模型被用于任何机密工作。高级国防领导人处理这场危机的方式不当，重新点燃了军方与人工智能行业之间长期存在的紧张关系。国防部无法承受疏远那些正在构建将决定未来战争形态的最强大技术的工程师们。军方需要获取尖端人工智能，但胁迫美国公司——正如五角大楼试图通过对Anthropic贴上“供应链风险”标签所做的那样——无助于鼓励合作。

2018年Google停止为国防部的早期机器学习和数据整合计划Project Maven工作后，五角大楼展开了一场魅力攻势。它制定了人工智能伦理原则——这是该部门负责任地采用人工智能的指导方针——这些原则不仅有助于解决许多人工智能研究人员对军事应用的担忧，也改善了军方使用人工智能的流程。今天的五角大楼领导层必须紧急改变方向，以缓解紧张局势并建立桥梁，而不是烧毁它们。

人工智能功能强大但有许多缺陷。当今的大型语言模型存在微妙的偏见、倾向于胡编乱造，并且会迎合用户——告诉用户人工智能认为用户想听的内容。有效使用人工智能需要认真应对这些局限性。可以独立在计算机和网络 上采取行动的人工智能代理将提高生产力。它们也可能出现严重问题。2026年4月，一个人工智能代理在九秒内删除了某家公司的整个数据库。（该人工智能代理事后很有风度地道歉了。）军方需要为人工智能系统和代理设置防护栏，并为人类用户提供培训，以确保人工智能不会导致破坏性错误。军方不仅需要赢得人工智能研究人员的支持，还需要真正倾听他们的意见，以便更好地理解这项技术的局限性。与行业合作对于建立基准、标准和测试流程至关重要，这些是军方成功使用人工智能所必需的。

华盛顿无法阻止人工智能的扩散。最后，武装部队必须在这个新时代更新其衡量军事实力的指标。海军计算舰艇数量；空军计算飞机数量。这些都是工业时代的指标。（陆军计算士兵数量——这是一个前工业时代的指标。）首先，规划者必须更好地将这些低成本无人机纳入统计。通常，这些载具被认为不够强大，不足以被视为飞机，但将它们排除在外可能会低估军事能力，并使规划偏向传统系统。但比这些数字更重要的是现在赋能和连接军事平台的数字组件的指标：传感器、雷达、计算机、网络和算法。国防部应该开始跟踪与人工智能相关的指标。这些可能包括其在任何时候在机密和非机密网络上可用的计算能力，以及这些计算能力的使用程度。它还可以跟踪每月活跃用户、人工智能模型上的令牌使用情况，以显示人工智能使用的广泛程度和频率，以及整个国防部可用数据的数量及其使用方式。这些数字将让规划者更详细地了解军事和文职人员使用人工智能的程度，以及需要在哪里进行额外投资或举措以加速采用。就像舰艇、航空母舰、飞机和军事人员的数量是国防部预算中的讨论要点一样，国防部可访问的H100等效GPU数量也应该成为讨论要点。要在人工智能领域领先，军方需要在人工智能能力方面进行投资。

军方还应该对人工智能的使用进行详细评估，以衡量这项技术是否提高了效率和准确性、降低了成本并加快了工作流程，同时也要确定可以应用于其他领域的经验教训。历史上充满了军事技术在颠覆性创新出现后难以调整和改革的警示案例。1588年，当英格兰和西班牙舰队交锋时，西班牙正处于其权力的顶峰。但英格兰海军更成功地利用了当时的新技术：大炮。相比之下，西班牙无敌舰队仍然围绕靠近并登上敌舰的战术设计，甲板上挤满了步兵。因此，庞大的西班牙舰队被彻底击败。西班牙无敌舰队战败后，英格兰与西班牙的战争又持续了16年，但西班牙海军的巅峰已过，其作为全球帝国的巅峰也已过去。

如果美国现在采取行动适应现代战争的变化，它就能保持世界领先的军事力量。但如果五角大楼未能将其行动推向必要的方向，它将被那些在适应新时代现实方面更加坚定和勇敢的竞争对手所超越。
articles.summary:
In its recent campaign against Iran, the United States dominated the skies using its traditional airpower. The U.S. military pounded Iranian targets, conducting over 13,000 strikes. That prowess and devastating firepower did not stop Iran from hitting back. Over the course of the 39-day conflict that began on February 28 and stopped on April 8, Iran launched over 2,200 missiles and 4,400 drones against countries in the region. At least eight U.S. aircraft were destroyed or damaged by Iranian attacks. Multiple U.S. radars were hit, and seven U.S. service members were killed. And at the time of this writing, the Iranian regime remains in place and maintains a stranglehold over the Strait of Hormuz. The United States has not achieved its objectives in the war, even though it is by every metric far more powerful than Iran.

The technological dominance that the U.S. military has long counted on to give it an advantage over competitors is waning. Unlike in past eras, when the United States maintained major leads in stealth and precision-guided weapons, the current age will not afford the United States an advantage in the technologies that are now transforming warfare: drones and artificial intelligence.

The conflict with Iran was the United States’ first taste of a new era of warfare. Emerging technologies are leveling the playing field between Washington and its adversaries. The diffusion of affordable drone technology and artificial intelligence capabilities is allowing smaller states and nonstate actors the chance to punch above their weight. Such adversaries can now hit U.S. rear bases, inflicting casualties and damaging expensive U.S. aircraft. Iranian missile attacks on U.S. bases in the Gulf destroyed one E-3 Sentry early warning aircraft. That loss is even greater than the airplane’s $300 million cost, since the U.S. fleet of E-3 aircraft is now down to only 15 and a replacement program is years away. Iranian missiles struck five KC-135 Stratotanker refueling aircraft, as well as multiple U.S. ground radars.

Drones have transformed not just the dynamics of warfare but also its economics. In the Gulf and elsewhere, low-cost air and naval drones and missiles can take out far more expensive assets. Ukraine used kamikaze drone boats and antiship missiles to eviscerate Russia’s Black Sea Fleet, sinking 13 ships after two years of war and damaging dozens more. A $300,000 drone boat can cripple a navy warship that costs hundreds of millions of dollars.

The United States still has the most powerful military in the world, but it is not yet prepared for a new age of warfare defined by these realities. It needs to produce more low-cost drones and interceptors, and it needs to better adapt to the imperatives of AI competition. Just as the military can’t amass airpower without building planes and can’t dominate the seas without floating ships, it can’t win in the AI age without harnessing data, buying computing power, and learning how best to use AI models. To maintain an edge on the battlefield, the U.S. military must find ways to efficiently assimilate these new technologies. That will require overcoming cultural and bureaucratic barriers within the armed services, forging closer relationships with the private sector, and finding new ways to assess military power. But if the U.S. military does not adapt in this way, it will increasingly find itself closely matched on the battlefield. After decades of dominance assured by its technological edge, the United States will be diminished because it has let its lead perilously slip.

GAME OF DRONES

The United States has long relied on technological innovation to gain an advantage over its adversaries. Early in the Cold War, U.S. defense planners counted on nuclear weapons to offset the Soviet military’s superior numbers in Europe. In the 1970s, the United States ushered in the information revolution in its military planning, and advances in semiconductors, computer networks, and satellites gave it a lead in stealth systems, precision-guided weapons, and GPS. These technologies proved invaluable in the 1990–91 Gulf War, when the United States systematically dismantled the Iraqi military. Their effect was even more impressive during the 2003 Iraq invasion, when U.S. forces seized Baghdad in just three weeks. In 2014, the Pentagon launched the “third offset” strategy, which sought to use robotics and AI to make up for the numerical superiority of Chinese and Russian forces. This strategy pushed the U.S. military to harness AI technology emerging from the commercial sector and persuaded U.S. officials that they could cement an enduring technological advantage over adversaries.

But this time, such a strategy will not work. The United States no longer has a discernible advantage in emerging technologies and will not be able to gain one.

Take, for instance, uncrewed vehicles. Cheap drones are widely available around the world, and the United States will not be able to prevent competitors from fielding them in large numbers. Iran has emerged in recent years as a major producer of cheap drones and has supplied thousands of drones to Russia for its war in Ukraine. Based on Iranian designs, Russia has produced tens of thousands more.

In theory, the United States should be able to produce a huge number of these weapons. Low-cost drones don’t rely on any special technology. But in practice, the U.S. military has struggled to field cheap drones in any significant numbers. Ukraine produces four million drones every year, while the U.S. Army is acquiring only 50,000.

Pentagon leaders in both the Biden and the Trump administrations have made the production of low-cost drones a priority, but structural problems have gotten in the way. Small military drones rely on technology originally developed for the commercial hobbyist market, which is dominated by the Chinese company DJI. The U.S. military rightly does not want to depend on military hardware from its chief competitor, so it ends up buying far more expensive U.S.-made drones (which still often use Chinese components).

Compute is analogous to manufacturing capacity in the industrial age.

More damning, the United States simply isn’t good at building anything cheaply, responding quickly, or scaling up rapidly. For decades, U.S. defense production has marched steadily up a cost curve toward ever more “exquisite” defense platforms—military parlance for advanced, expensive, and low-volume weapons. Drones, by contrast, have tilted the military landscape toward low-cost, attritable (or expendable) weapons that can be produced in great quantity.

The United States has been slow to adapt. The Defense Department’s 2023 Replicator initiative aimed to field thousands of low-cost autonomous systems quickly but yielded only hundreds. The current Pentagon leadership has announced plans to expand the production of low-cost drones, committing over $1 billion to produce 340,000 drones by 2027. The army has set an even more ambitious goal of producing at least a million drones by 2028. To achieve these goals, the military will need to deliver consistent and substantial funding to build an industrial base for small drones that does not yet exist at significant scale.

But drone technology is not standing still. Soon, these vehicles will be able to operate with greater autonomy and in closer coordination with other machines. Most drones today are remotely piloted or use simple automation, such as by following designated waypoints or returning to base if they lose the connection to a human pilot. Ukraine has become a testing ground for more sophisticated autonomous features. For instance, many Ukrainian drones have autonomous terminal guidance, allowing the uncrewed aircraft to navigate several hundred meters on its own to the target if enemy jamming breaks the communications link between the machine and the human pilot. Ukraine is also producing long-range strike drones that can travel up to 600 miles and autonomously navigate without GPS by matching images from onboard cameras to preloaded satellite imagery. These innovations will be adopted far beyond Ukraine. More countries and nonstate actors will soon possess similar drones that can hit targets even when adversaries can block communications and prevent the drone from accessing GPS. Drones will be equipped with ever more sophisticated autonomous guidance systems that will allow them to search wide areas and identify and attack targets all on their own.

These advances will change warfare in profound ways. What today are simple drones will become tomorrow’s intelligent swarms: thousands of drones reacting in real time to changing conditions on the battlefield. Swarms will be used to hunt mobile targets, conduct simultaneous attacks to overwhelm defenses, and build communications and logistics networks that are resilient to enemy jamming, disruption, or attacks. Autonomous robot swarms will be able to act with a speed, coordination, and dynamism that human pilots could never replicate.

Taking full advantage of drone swarms will require radically rethinking military command and control, organizational structures, and how human commanders direct military forces on the battlefield. Military operators won’t pilot drones directly. They will command entire swarms of hundreds or thousands of drones, with the drones themselves autonomously coordinating their behavior. Militaries will need to figure out what types of directions to give swarms and how autonomous drones should coordinate among themselves. That will require quite a change from traditional models of command in the military, replacing hierarchical structures with more decentralized ones.

Drones are already changing dynamics on the battlefield in ways that the United States has not yet grappled with. In the war in Ukraine, for instance, persistent drones overhead have made it hard for either side to mass forces. Drones are now responsible for the majority of Russian casualties, supplanting artillery. The war in Iran has shown how drones have made bases far from the frontlines vulnerable. The U.S. military will have to adapt to this new reality, investing more heavily in camouflage, decoys, and other methods of hiding from detection and dispersing forces to reduce risk.

The United States also needs more cost-effective ways to defend against the vast numbers of missiles and cheap drones that adversaries can launch. Missile defense has come a long way in the 35 years since the Gulf War, when U.S. Patriot batteries were almost entirely ineffective in shooting down Iraqi Scud missiles targeting Israel. But offensive missile technology has evolved, too, and the threat from drones has mushroomed. The net effect has been that the United States has lost ground despite running faster. Missile defenses today are effective but costly. The United States, Israel, and the Gulf countries shot down 1,700 Iranian ballistic missiles and drones since the end of February, but the cost-exchange ratio has heavily favored Iran. Intercepting a $35,000 (or, according to some recent estimates, $7,000) Shahed drone with a $4 million Patriot missile will only ever be a Pyrrhic victory. Washington sees the losses mounting on the balance sheet.

The American military doesn’t have enough missile interceptors, and the war against Iran has badly depleted U.S. stockpiles. Just since the war began, the United States has used roughly half of its Patriot missiles and between 50 and 80 percent of its THAAD interceptor missiles. The Trump administration is taking steps to expand production capacity, but it will take years to replenish the losses. The depletion of these stockpiles will leave U.S. forces vulnerable not only in the Middle East but also in Asia and Europe.

As with low-cost drones, the Pentagon is taking steps to develop and scale production of low-cost interceptors. U.S. Coyote drone interceptors cost around $125,000 apiece, while Merops drone interceptors cost around $15,000 each, a major improvement over million-dollar missiles. Washington will need to scale up production of these cheaper interceptors just to keep pace with the growing threat.

NEXT TOP MODEL

AI will bring even more sweeping changes to warfare. Although the United States is home to the world’s leading AI companies, advances in the field will further accelerate the erosion of American military technological superiority. Washington is gripped by the supposed “AI race” between the United States and China, but the reality today is essentially technological parity.

Chinese AI models trail leading American ones by only a few months. Chinese companies such as DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax effectively piggyback on U.S. models, using them to train their own models at a fraction of the cost. Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google have all caught and reported foreign competitors that were conducting large-scale efforts to extract information from American models in violation of those models’ terms of service. Chinese companies make up for their limited access to advanced AI chips—constrained by U.S. export controls—by copying the gains made by U.S. firms that possess the most powerful and advanced chips. This technique, called adversarial distillation, functionally negates the American advantage in the most cutting-edge AI capabilities.

Another area in which the United States has until recently enjoyed an edge is in using AI to transform its intelligence analysis and operational planning. Large language models are integrated into Palantir’s Maven Smart System, which pools intelligence from multiple sources into a single interface for analysts to assess the battle space. AI allows intelligence analysts and planners to synthesize vast amounts of data and plan strikes. The Israeli military reportedly used machine learning systems to process data and recommend targets for strikes in Gaza, but the U.S. military’s operations against Iran are likely the first significant use of large language models on the battlefield. In Iran, where U.S. warplanes have frequently been redirected to new targets midflight, the U.S. military has used AI to prioritize targets and build strike packages amid a fluid and dynamic battle space.

America’s technological dominance is waning.

But within a few months, China’s military will have access to AI models with the same capabilities. In fact, every military and nonstate group on the planet will have access to these kinds of tools; after all, AI is not the closely guarded secret of particular governments, but the work of the commercial sector, and such innovations proliferate worldwide fairly quickly. Even though leading American companies are willing to work with the U.S. military, AI technology spreads faster than the military can reasonably integrate and adopt it, never mind use it to transform operations. Indeed, what matters more for militaries is not which country first develops a new AI tool or capability, but which military can first adopt it.

During periods of disruptive technological change, what determines a military’s relative success is how well it employs new technology. In the early twentieth century, for instance, all the leading military powers of the age had access to new weapons such as tanks, submarines, and airplanes. The challenge was figuring out how best to use them.

The period between World War I and World War II saw militaries experiment with new technologies and invent new organizational structures, doctrines, and training to harness these weapons. The United Kingdom was the first to innovate with aircraft carriers but fell behind Japan and the United States in the run-up to World War II. British aircraft technology was among the most advanced, but cultural and bureaucratic obstacles within the British military, such as its misguided decision to give responsibility for naval aviation to the Royal Air Force rather than the navy, slowed technological adoption.

That matters because methods, more than cutting-edge equipment and systems, make the difference on the battlefield. After all, most wars are fought between adversaries that have approximate technological parity. In a study of land wars from 1956 to 1992, the scholar Stephen Biddle found that the time gap between adversaries in military technology was on average less than three years.

AT THE BLEEDING EDGE

Restraining China’s computing power is essential to edging out Beijing in AI adoption and allowing the U.S. military to use AI more effectively, even if China has access to AI models with the same capabilities. Computing power is essential for deploying AI at scale. Using the most advanced AI models takes a lot of energy and computing power, and tech companies are pouring hundreds of billions of dollars into building massive data centers to meet AI demand. Today, computing power is roughly analogous to manufacturing capacity during the industrial age. Just as a country’s manufacturing capacity determined its economic growth and military prowess, aggregate “compute” will determine a country’s AI power—and, consequentially, its strength.

The most powerful tool the United States has to slow China’s progress in AI is export controls that prevent Chinese firms from procuring advanced chips and semiconductor manufacturing equipment. Chips are essential for training and using the most advanced AI models, and U.S. companies occupy key chokepoints in the chip production supply chain.

Under the first Trump administration and the Biden administration, the U.S. government steadily ratcheted up export controls on advanced AI chips and chip-making equipment to China. But in January 2026, the Trump administration reversed course and approved Nvidia’s H200 chip for sale to China. As of April 2026, the chips had not yet been transferred to China, even though the Commerce Department had issued licenses for limited quantities and Nvidia had received orders from Chinese customers. Given overall constraints in the supply of chips for AI development and surging demand in the United States, every chip sold to China represents a loss for Washington and a boon for Beijing. The Trump administration should reinstate the ban on advanced AI chips to China rather than give up the United States’ lead to a strategic competitor.

The Trump administration should also work with Japan and the Netherlands to tighten export controls on chip-making equipment to China. Advanced chip fabrication plants rely on technology from Japan, the Netherlands, and the United States. China is desperately trying to increase its domestic semiconductor manufacturing capacity to reduce its dependence on foreign chips. But without access to critical chip-making equipment, China will not be able to produce leading-edge chips. The first Trump administration put significant pressure on the Netherlands to halt sales of extreme ultraviolet lithography equipment to China, machines that are needed to make the most advanced chips. China has nevertheless continued to make progress by using older, deep ultraviolet immersion lithography technology that is not restricted.

Of course, trying to restrict China’s access to hardware, such as chips and chip-making equipment, will do little to limit its gains from adversarial distillation. The U.S. government should also work with AI companies to crack down on foreign competitors that extract the capabilities of American models. Congress should pass legislation to protect U.S. companies from antitrust liability when they share information about adversarial distillation with one another, similar to existing legislation addressing cyberthreats. Better cooperation among American AI firms could improve defenses against adversarial distillation by sharing best practices and threat information. And Washington should sanction Chinese entities involved in illicitly extracting the capabilities of AI models belonging to U.S. firms. Sanctioning specific Chinese companies would prohibit U.S. firms from working with them and, in the most extreme case, cut the offending Chinese companies off from the global financial system.

In some cases, AI labs themselves may want to keep some of the most advanced AI capabilities from public release, which could slow proliferation. OpenAI and Anthropic have taken this approach when delaying the release of their latest models, such as Anthropic’s Mythos, out of fear that bad actors could use them for offensive cyberattacks. Anthropic has partnered with several leading technology companies in Project Glasswing to use Anthropic’s AI model to find and patch cyber-vulnerabilities before more dangerous capabilities proliferate. OpenAI has created a “trusted access” program that allows thousands of verified cybersecurity experts to access OpenAI’s tools for cyberdefense.

These approaches can give cybersecurity professionals a head start in fending off the dangerous AI capabilities that are coming, but the clock is ticking. As of October 2025, the AI research group Epoch AI assessed that the most capable open-weight models—that is, models available for anyone to download—trailed state-of-the-art models by only three months. Restricting release will slow proliferation by making adversarial distillation more challenging, but it will not be a permanent solution. Jack Clark, the co-founder of Anthropic, estimated in April 2026 that what counts as state-of-the-art AI cyber-capabilities today will be broadly available and openly sourced within 12 to 18 months.

Methods, more than equipment, make the difference on the battlefield.

Washington cannot halt the proliferation of AI capabilities, but it can still gain a little bit of an edge. Stretching a three-month lead into 18 months buys more time for cybersecurity experts and the U.S. military to adopt the latest AI technologies. In that sense, the right approach to technology won’t give the United States an enduring advantage, but it will offer Washington a small lead in what will be a constant race.

The United States needs to use that time to innovate, experiment with AI, and adapt its own organizations and doctrine to make the most of the latest technology. Doing so will require a mindset shift, steering away from the ponderous, deliberate approach the U.S. military usually takes in peacetime to a wartime approach based on swift iteration and adaptation. The U.S. military rapidly revised its practices during the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, quickly fielding equipment and modifying tactics to counter the threat from improvised explosive devices and to fly drones to surveil insurgents. Traditional bureaucratic Pentagon processes for establishing requirements for military systems, budgeting costs, and procuring technologies won’t keep pace with AI and stay ahead of adversaries. Motivated by an existential sense of urgency, Ukraine has scaled production to four million drones a year. With 140 times Ukraine’s GDP, the United States should be able to come close to that number. Although it took years for the Pentagon to invest sufficiently in armored vehicles to seriously counter the threat from roadside bombs in Iraq and Afghanistan, once Secretary of Defense Robert Gates made it a priority in 2007, the military fielded 10,000 armored vehicles in about a year and a half.

Fortunately, the current leadership in the Pentagon is willing to break the mold. The Department of Defense has put large language models on its classified and unclassified networks, giving three million military and civilian users across the defense establishment access to AI models. Pentagon leadership is also expanding the number of models available across networks, giving employees access to a diversity of AI platforms. Initial signs are positive. The Defense Department has reported that over one million users have used AI models. But the department will have to do more to create the right bureaucratic and cultural incentives for adoption. This includes giving employees the freedom to experiment with AI and accepting failure and mistakes.

The department’s AI strategy, released in January, emphasized the importance of speed. To help cut through red tape, the strategy established a monthly “barrier removal board” to waive nonlegislative restrictions that might impede AI adoption. To allow greater access to data, the strategy directed that data be shared with authorized users and that any denial of a request for data be justified within seven days. These are welcome moves to speed up the Pentagon. But speed alone won’t be enough.

IDENTITY CRISIS

Some of the biggest obstacles to fully harnessing the advantages of new technology are cultural. Technological advances require new ways of waging war, and these can sometimes challenge ingrained habits and deeply held identities within the military services. The U.S. Navy resisted the transition from sail to steam in the nineteenth century and even regressed on steam adoption after the Civil War. Debates about how to most effectively use tanks persisted in the U.S. Army throughout World War II. As late as 1943, Lieutenant General Lesley McNair, the commander of Army Ground Forces, wrote a memo to General George Marshall, the army chief of staff, arguing that Germany’s blitzkrieg through France three years earlier was an aberration, and that the proper role of tanks was to support the infantry, not lead an armored assault on their own.

Today’s military services are no less hidebound. Each service’s culture and conception of airpower shape how it has adopted drones. The army was the first to embrace more automated flight controls, including for takeoff and landing, and to use enlisted personnel as drone controllers. The air force resisted these innovations, which challenged its conception of drone controllers as “pilots.” Yet the air force was innovative in piloting drones from bases in the continental United States while the army chose to forward deploy drone operators to Iraq and Afghanistan, a much less efficient use of personnel. Concentrating drone operators on bases in the United States allows them to operate drones continuously, while the army’s policy of forward-deploying drone operators during the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan meant roughly two-thirds of army drone operators were stateside between deployments and not flying. But in the army’s view, soldiers shouldn’t telecommute to war.

Enthusiasm for uncrewed and robotic systems has varied widely in the navy. The navy’s submarine force has largely embraced undersea robotic vehicles, which are a complement to submarines, not a substitute for them. In naval aviation, however, aircraft carrier deck space is limited. Each drone added to a carrier deck supplants a traditional crewed fighter aircraft. Even though a stealthy combat drone could dramatically extend the carrier’s reach, the navy downgraded its carrier-based drones to tanker aircraft that would transport gas to support, not replace, crewed fighter aircraft. In doing so to save pilots’ jobs, the navy chose to sacrifice the aircraft carrier’s reach and striking power.

Artificial intelligence presents an even greater challenge to the self-images of military services than do drones. AI raises fundamental questions about the roles of humans and machines. The same fears about AI taking jobs across society will play out in the military, where service members’ identities are strongly connected to the tasks they perform—so strongly that they sometimes persist even after technology has long rendered a task obsolete. Naval personnel are still called “sailors” even though they no longer climb masts, lower or raise sails, or handle rigging. The army still has soldiers who identify as “cavalry” even though they no longer ride horses. These identities persist as historical artifacts even as the jobs of military personnel change—and the same could happen as AI transforms the armed services. But the history of military technological adoption, from steam-powered ships to tanks to drones, suggests that identity and culture can be powerful forces preventing militaries from unlocking the true benefits of new technologies.

SINKING THE ARMADA

Another force in the United States is essential to ensuring the country’s military technological lead: the private sector. Adopting AI that works will require deep partnership with the broader industry, the companies developing AI, and third-party evaluators who are experts in AI capabilities and limitations. To do so, Pentagon leadership will need to repair relationships with Silicon Valley that have grown strained in recent months by the falling out with Anthropic over the terms of its contract with the Defense Department—the Pentagon insisted it wanted unrestricted access to Anthropic’s technology for “any lawful use,” while Anthropic wanted to put guardrails around the potential use of its technology for domestic mass surveillance and for powering fully autonomous weapons. At stake is much more than just the military’s ties to one company. The public dispute has fed a backlash among AI engineers, who are now increasingly opposed to working with the military. Over 1,000 employees at Google and OpenAI signed an open letter urging their companies to “stand together to continue to refuse the Department of War’s current demands.” In April 2026, more than 600 Google employees signed an open letter urging the company not to allow its AI models to be used for any classified work at all. Senior defense leaders have mismanaged this crisis and reignited long-standing tensions between the military and the AI industry.

The Defense Department cannot afford to alienate the engineers who are building the most powerful technology that will shape the future of war. The military must have access to leading-edge AI, but coercing U.S. companies, as the Pentagon tried to do by labeling Anthropic a “supply chain risk,” won’t help encourage collaboration. After Google discontinued work on the Defense Department’s early machine-learning and data-integration initiative known as Project Maven in 2018, the Pentagon went on a charm offensive. It produced the AI Ethical Principles, the department’s guidelines for responsibly adopting AI, that not only helped address many AI researchers’ concerns about the military applications of their work but also improved the military’s processes for using AI. Today’s Pentagon leadership must urgently change course to defuse tensions and build bridges, not burn them.

AI is powerful but has many flaws. Large language models today have subtle biases, tend to make things up, and engage in sycophancy—telling the user what the AI believes the user wants to hear. The effective use of AI requires grappling seriously with these limitations. AI agents, which can take independent actions on computers and networks, will accelerate productivity. They can also go badly awry. In April 2026, an AI agent deleted one company’s entire database in nine seconds. (The AI agent had the grace to apologize afterward.) The military will need to set guardrails for AI systems and agents, as well as provide training for human users to ensure that AI does not lead to damaging mistakes. The military needs not just to win over AI researchers but to actually listen to them to better understand the technology’s limitations. Partnership with industry is essential to establishing the benchmarks, standards, and testing processes needed to make the military’s use of AI successful.

Washington cannot halt the proliferation of AI.

Finally, the armed forces must update its metrics for measuring military power in this new era. The navy counts the number of ships; the air force, the number of aircraft. These are industrial-age metrics. (The army counts the number of soldiers—a pre-industrial metric.) For one thing, planners must do a better job of factoring low-cost drones into these counts. Often, these vehicles are not considered powerful enough to count as aircraft, but excluding them risks understating military capacity and skewing planning toward legacy systems.

But far more important than these figures now are measures of the digital components that empower and connect military platforms: sensors, radars, computers, networks, and algorithms. The Defense Department should begin tracking AI-relevant metrics. These could include the amount of computing power it has available at any one time on classified and unclassified networks and how much that computing power is being used. It could also track active monthly users, token usage on AI models to show how widespread and frequent AI use is, and the amount of data available across the Defense Department and how it is being used. These figures would give planners a more detailed understanding of the extent to which military and civilian workers are using AI and where additional investments or initiatives are needed to accelerate adoption. Just as the number of ships, aircraft carriers, planes, and service members are points of discussion in the Defense Department budget, so, too, should the number of H100-equivalent GPUs the department has access to. To lead in AI, the military will need to invest in AI power. The military should also conduct detailed assessments of the use of AI to measure whether the technology has increased efficiency and accuracy, improved costs, and accelerated workflows, as well as to determine what lessons can be applied to other applications.

History is full of cautionary tales of militaries that struggled to shift and reform after the advent of disruptive technologies. When English and Spanish fleets clashed in 1588, Spain was at the height of its power. But the English navy had more successfully capitalized on the new technology of the day: cannons. The Spanish Armada, by contrast, was still designed around the imperative of closing with and boarding enemy ships, their decks packed with infantry. As a result, the vast Spanish fleet was hopelessly outgunned and was defeated. The war between England and Spain dragged on for 16 more years after the defeat of the Spanish Armada, but the peak of Spain’s naval power had passed, as had the peak of Spain’s power as a global empire.

The United States can remain the world’s leading military if it acts now to adapt to the changing contours of modern warfare. But if the Pentagon fails to push its operations in the necessary directions, it will be eclipsed by competitors that are more dogged and intrepid in adjusting to the realities of a new age.
